coredata.dc:title: Photophysics and luminescence quenching of carbon dots derived from lemon juice and glycerol
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Gharat, Poojan Milan
Pal, Haridas
Dutta Choudhury, Sharmistha
coredata.dc:description: During the past decade, carbon dots have emerged as a fascinating class of luminescent nanomaterials with versatile application potentials in bioimaging, labeling, photocatalysis and optoelectronics. Currently, intensive research is concentrated on understanding the intriguing optical properties of these promising materials and their utility as luminescence sensors. In this article, we describe the photoluminescence of carbon dots obtained from a bioresource (lemon juice) and from a small molecule precursor (glycerol), especially the quenching of their emission by nitrobenzene and Hg2+ ions, as representative cases. Stern-Volmer analysis using steady-state and time-resolved emission measurements, suggests the involvement of both transient quenching and dynamic quenching mechanisms in the interaction of the carbon dots with nitrobenzene. The radius of the quenching sphere is estimated to be slightly greater than the contact distances between the respective carbon dots and nitrobenzene, which is in reasonable agreement with the “sphere of action” model for transient quenching. In the interaction with Hg2+ ions, electrostatic attraction plays a major role, and the quenching mechanism involves predominantly static and dynamic quenching. The static quenching constant matches well with the binding constant of the carbon dots with the metal ion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Photoluminescence
Stern-Volmer
Static quenching
Dynamic quenching
Transient quenching

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Absorption spectra of G-CD in aqueous medium. Inset shows photographs of G-CD upon illumination with UV light (I) and under daylight (II). (B) Emission spectra of G-CD on excitation at 320 nm, 340 nm, 360 nm, 380 nm, 400 nm, 420 nm and 440 nm (1–7). (C) Intensity decay trace of G-CD in aqueous medium monitored at 450 nm, excitation wavelength is 374 nm, IRF is the instrument response function.

(A) PL spectra of G-CD in aqueous medium in the presence of different concentrations of NBz (1–6)/mM: 0, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 15; excitation wavelength is 360 nm. (B) PL decay traces of G-CD in aqueous medium monitored at 450 nm, in the presence of different concentrations of NBz (1–4)/mM: 0, 2.5, 5, 10; excitation wavelength is 374 nm, IRF is the instrument response function. (C) Stern-Volmer plots for the G-CD-NBz system, following both the intensity and lifetime quenching. (D) Fitted curves for the steady-state quenching of G-CD-NBz, following Eq. (6) (green) and Eq. (7) (red). (E) Observed linear correlation of
NBz
I 0
I
− 1
versus [NBz]1/3 for the steady-state quenching of G-CD by NBz.

(A) PL spectra of L-CD in aqueous medium in the presence of different concentrations of NBz (1–6)/mM: 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2.5, 4.5; excitation wavelength is 360 nm. (B) PL decay traces of L-CD in aqueous medium monitored at 450 nm, in the presence of different concentrations of NBz (1–4)/mM: 0, 2.5, 3.5, 4.5; excitation wavelength is 374 nm, IRF is the instrument response function. (C) Stern-Volmer plots for the L-CD-NBz system, following both the intensity and lifetime quenching. (D) Fitted curves for the steady-state quenching of L-CD-NBz, following Eq. (6) (green) and Eq. (7) (red). (E) Observed linear correlation of
NBz
I 0
I
− 1
versus [NBz]1/3 for the steady-state quenching of L-CD by NBz.

(A) PL spectra of L-CD in aqueous medium (pH ~8.9) in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ (1–6)/mM: 0, 0.15, 0.19, 0.37, 0.77, 1.82; excitation wavelength is 320 nm. (B) PL decay traces of L-CD in aqueous medium monitored at 450 nm, in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ (1–4)/mM: 0, 0.29, 0.77, 1.82; excitation wavelength is 374 nm, IRF is the instrument response function. (C) Stern-Volmer plots for the L-CD-Hg2+ system, following both the intensity and lifetime quenching. (D) Fitted curves for the steady-state quenching data according to Eq. (6) (green) and Eq. (7) (red). (E) Plot of
Hg
2 +
I 0
I
− 1
versus [Hg 2+]1/3; no linear correlation is detected for the L-CD-Hg2+ system.
Double logarithmic plot for the quenching of L-CD by Hg2+ at pH 8.9 and the fitted curve according to Eq. (9).
PL decay parameters a at 450 nm for G-CD in the presence of different concentrations of nitrobenzene (NBz), excitation wavelength is 374 nm.
PL decay parameters a at 450 nm for L-CD in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ at pH 8.9, excitation wavelength is 374 nm.

head.ce:title: Photophysics and luminescence quenching of carbon dots derived from lemon juice and glycerol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Radiation & Photochemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai 400085, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Homi Bhabha National Institute, Training School Complex, Anushaktinagar, Mumbai 400094, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Radiation & Photochemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai 400085, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: During the past decade, carbon dots have emerged as a fascinating class of luminescent nanomaterials with versatile application potentials in bioimaging, labeling, photocatalysis and optoelectronics. Currently, intensive research is concentrated on understanding the intriguing optical properties of these promising materials and their utility as luminescence sensors. In this article, we describe the photoluminescence of carbon dots obtained from a bioresource (lemon juice) and from a small molecule precursor (glycerol), especially the quenching of their emission by nitrobenzene and Hg2+ ions, as representative cases. Stern-Volmer analysis using steady-state and time-resolved emission measurements, suggests the involvement of both transient quenching and dynamic quenching mechanisms in the interaction of the carbon dots with nitrobenzene. The radius of the quenching sphere is estimated to be slightly greater than the contact distances between the respective carbon dots and nitrobenzene, which is in reasonable agreement with the “sphere of action” model for transient quenching. In the interaction with Hg2+ ions, electrostatic attraction plays a major role, and the quenching mechanism involves predominantly static and dynamic quenching. The static quenching constant matches well with the binding constant of the carbon dots with the metal ion.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent carbon dots have attracted tremendous research attention due to their great potential in biological labeling, imaging, photocatalysis and optoelectronic device applications [1–5]. Compared to traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots have better photostability, low toxicity, good water solubility and excellent biocompatibility. They were first isolated by Xu and co-workers in 2004 as a byproduct of crude arc discharge soot purification [6]. Since then, a large number of methods for preparing carbon dots have been developed [7]. The carbon dots are easy to synthesize in large scale using both top-down or bottom-up approaches and can be readily surface functionalized. Suitable top-down methods involve chemical or mechanical disruption of bulk graphitic material, laser ablation or application of voltage to graphitic electrodes [3]. Bottom-up methods include the hydrothermal or microwave treatment of a range of precursors, like, citric acid, saccharides, ethylene glycol, and even the abundantly available biomass, such as, eggs, fruit peels and other organic wastes [3,7].

Structurally, carbon dots are spherical nanoparticles that are considered to be made up of sp2 hybridized carbon domains surrounded by sp3 hybridized carbons [3,8]. Their external surfaces are attached with different functional groups, like carboxyl, hydroxyl, epoxy or amino, depending on the precursors and preparation methods. In recent years, there is rising evidence that molecular chromophores or polyaromatic hydrocarbons are also generated and associated with the carbon dot structures during bottom-up synthesis procedures [9–12].

Interestingly, irrespective of the precursors used or the synthetic procedures involved, the optical properties of most carbon dots have considerable similarities. One intriguing feature observed for many carbon dots is that their photoluminescence (PL) spectra depend on the excitation wavelength [3,4,13,14]. The PL of carbon dots is also sensitive to the pH of the solution and the polarity of the solvent medium [15–17]. The absorption/emission patterns of all carbon dots are, however, not exactly the same. These differences pose a challenge for the detailed understanding of their photophysical properties. There are several interpretations for the observed luminescence of carbon dots, which include, electronic transition between the quantum confined core, participation of surface trap states and presence of molecular fluorophores or multichromophoric units either within or along with individual carbon dots [2,5,13,18,19]. A large number of theoretical studies on the relation of energy gaps and absorption/emission spectra of carbon dots with the sizes of their aromatic domains, have also been published [18,20,21].

In this paper we describe the PL properties of carbon dots prepared from a bioresource (lemon juice) and from a small molecule precursor (glycerol). The absorption and emission spectra that are characteristics of carbon dot systems, are observed for these materials. Luminescence quenching is detected on addition of Hg2+ or nitrobenzene (NBz). The suitability of the prepared carbon dots for fluorescence imaging of live Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cells is also discussed.

The carbon dot sample, L-CD, was prepared by direct thermal decomposition of lemon juice, as described in our earlier publication [15]. Briefly, 25 ml of filtered lemon juice was taken in a glass beaker and heated at 100 °C for 90 min, till the color of the sample changed to orange brown. The liquid was then centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The resulting supernatant was filtered and the L-CD sample dispersed in the filtrate was stored for further use. Since citric acid is among the most commonly used reagents for carbon dot synthesis [12,22,23], lemon juice was considered as an alternative “green” precursor. Similarly, carbohydrates and glycerol, are also widely used for carbon dot synthesis, and accordingly, the carbon dot sample, G-CD, was prepared from glycerol following the simple one-step, microwave synthesis protocol reported in the literature [24]. Briefly, 70% (v/v) glycerol in 7 mM phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4) was heated in a microwave oven (720 W) for 14 min, to obtain a golden-brown suspension of the G-CD sample. The experimental solutions for the photophysical studies were prepared by directly diluting small aliquots of L-CD or G-CD stock samples, in aqueous medium. The pH of the resulting aqueous dispersions was in the range of 2.6–3. For the quenching studies with Hg2+, the pH of the solutions was increased to 8.9 by using dilute NaOH solution, and measured using a pH-meter, model CL/46 from Toshcon, India.

Absorption spectra were recorded with a Jasco UV–vis spectrophotometer (model V-650) and steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with a Hitachi spectrofluorimeter (F-4510). The PL quantum yield was estimated by a comparative method, using quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as the reference (φref = 0.57) [25]. Time-resolved emission measurements were carried out using a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon IBH, UK). The samples were excited with a nano-LED source (374 nm, repetition rate of 1 MHz) and the emission was detected at magic angle configuration, using a PMT based detection module (model TBX4). The instrument response function (IRF) for the present setup is ∼185 ps. The PL decays were analysed by reconvolution method [25], considering a multi-exponential decay function (Eq. (1)).
(1)
I
t
=
∑ i
a i
exp
− t
τ i

Here a i is the absolute pre-exponential factor for the ith lifetime component, τ i. The relative contribution of each lifetime component was calculated as,
(2)
A i
=
a i
τ i
/
∑ i
a i
τ i
and the average lifetime, <τ>, was estimated as [25],
(3)
∑
A i
τ i

The quality of the fits was judged by the reduced chi-square (χ2) values and the distribution of the weighted residuals among the data channels [25]. For a good fit, the χ2 value was close to unity and the weighted residuals were distributed randomly around zero among the data channels.

Fig. 1A shows the representative absorption spectrum of G-CD in aqueous medium. The characteristic bands around 220 nm and 270 nm are generally attributed to the electronic transitions of the carbogenic core and the molecular states of the carbon dots, respectively. The transitions of the carbogenic core are usually π-π* in nature while the transitions of the molecular state can be both n-π* and π-π* in nature [5,13,14,18,19]. A weak absorption tail extending to longer wavelengths is also observed for G-CD and is considered to be due to the surface state transitions in the carbon dots [26,27]. The inset of Fig. 1A shows photographs of the G-CD sample upon illumination with UV light and under normal daylight conditions. While no luminescence is visually detectable under daylight conditions (cf. Inset II, Fig. 1A), typical blue luminescence is observed on UV excitation (cf. Inset I, Fig. 1A). The PL quantum yield on excitation at 360 nm is determined to be 0.07.

Fig. 1B shows the PL spectra of G-CD on excitation at different wavelengths. A clear red shift in the emission maximum, accompanied by a reduction in the emission intensity, is observed on increasing the excitation wavelength. As discussed in the introduction, the red shift in the emission maximum of carbon dots with increase in the excitation wavelength, is a common trait for many carbon dot systems [3,4,13,14,18]. The excitation wavelength dependent PL of carbon dots is considered to be an indication of optical heterogeneity in the carbon dots. Luminescence centers of specific energy levels are photoselected by the chosen excitation wavelength and accordingly, show their corresponding emission spectra and intensities [14,18]. The PL decay trace of G-CD (Fig. 1C) is multi-exponential in nature and a tri-exponential analysis gives a reasonably good fit with three lifetime components; 0.2 ns, 2.8 ns and 11 ns, which is in agreement with the optical heterogeneity. It has been suggested that the different lifetime components are related to different luminescence centers and different deactivation pathways of the carbon dots [18]. The average PL lifetime of G-CD is estimated to be 5.7 ns. For a comparison, it may be mentioned that Strauss et al. have reported an average PL lifetime of 6.1 ns for carbon dots synthesized from citric acid and urea [18], Khan et al. have reported average PL lifetimes of 6.4 and 3.5 ns for two types of carbon dots synthesized from chitosan and polyethylene glycol [28], while Ge et al. have reported PL lifetimes in the range of 6–14 ns for different kinds of carbon dots obtained from carbon soot and by the carbonization of molecular precursors [29].

The absorption and emission features of L-CD are grossly similar to those of G-CD, and display the usual characteristics of the carbon dot systems, as shown in Fig. S1, ESM. The PL quantum yield of L-CD on excitation at 360 nm is determined to be 0.024. Unlike G-CD, however, the PL spectra of L-CD are observed to be apparently independent of the excitation wavelength [15]. Nevertheless, the fluorescence decay trace of L-CD is multi-exponential in nature with three lifetime components; 0.04 ns, 1.6 ns and 5.5 ns, and an average lifetime of 4.3 ns [15]. The multi-exponential PL decay suggests the presence of multiple optical centers in L-CD as well. Possibly, the emission spectra of the different luminescence centers in this carbon dot are overlapped with each other, and as a result, there is no observable red shift in the PL of L-CD with increase in the excitation wavelength.

Next, we investigated the effects of some selected organic and inorganic analytes on the luminescence properties of the prepared carbon dots, to understand their interaction mechanisms. In this regard, we have chosen a nitroaromatic compound, nitrobenzene (NBz), and a heavy metal ion, Hg2+, as representative examples, considering that carbon dot systems are being extensively used for the sensing of these types of analytes [30–35]. The present studies with Hg2+ are in extension of our previous results obtained with the L-CD system [15].

Fig. 2A shows the changes in the PL spectra of G-CD, with the addition of gradually increasing concentrations of NBz. As indicated by the figure, the addition of NBz leads to a significant decrease in the PL intensity. Concomitant with the quenching in the PL intensity, the PL decay traces are also observed to become faster with the increasing concentration of NBz (Fig. 2B). The PL decay parameters of G-CD in the presence of different concentrations of NBz are presented in Table 1 . Since NBz is a good electron acceptor, it is anticipated that electron transfer interaction between G-CD and the NBz quencher is the major mechanism for the observed luminescence quenching [30,36]. It may be mentioned here that the carbon dots have been reported to act either as good electron donors or as good electron acceptors in the presence of suitable redox species [18].

The interaction between G-CD and the quencher, NBz, was analysed following the standard Stern-Volmer (SV) relationships [25] as,
(4)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K SV
Q
= 1 +
k q
< τ
> 0
Q
(5)
< τ
> 0
< τ >
= 1 +
K SV
Q
= 1 +
k q
< τ
> 0
Q

Here, I 0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of the quencher, Q, I is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of quencher, <τ>0 is the average lifetime in the absence of Q, <τ> is the average lifetime in the presence of quencher, K SV is the SV quenching constant and is equal to “k
q <τ>0”; k
q being the bimolecular quenching rate constant [25].

The SV plots for the G-CD-NBz system following Eqs. (4) and (5) are depicted in Fig. 2C. It is seen that although the SV plot based on the time-resolved quenching is linear, the SV plot based on the steady-state PL quenching shows considerable deviation from linear behavior. The upward curvature in the SV plot based on the PL intensities is an indication for the occurrence of either static or transient quenching, in addition to dynamic quenching, for the G-CD-NBz system [25,37,38]. From the slope of the linear portion of the steady-state SV plot, the bimolecular quenching rate constant, k q,intensity, is determined to be 7.1 × 1010 M−1 s−1, while from the time-resolved quenching data, the bimolecular quenching rate constant, k q,lifetime, is determined to be 1.1 × 1010 M−1 s−1. The k q,lifetime value is quite close to the bimolecular diffusion controlled rate expected in aqueous medium (~1 × 1010 M−1 s−1). The higher value of k q,intensity compared to k q,lifetime, is understandably due to the presence of static or transient quenching effects, which is analysed in further detail below.

For any emitter-quencher system, the occurrence of simultaneous static and dynamic quenching is possible, if, in addition to the diffusional encounters of the quencher with the excited state of the emitter (dynamic quenching), there is also some complex formation between the quencher and the emitter in the ground state itself (static quenching). In this situation, the SV Eq. (4) is modified as,
(6)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K D
+
K S
Q
+
K D
K S
Q
2
where K D and K
S are the dynamic and static quenching constants, respectively [25]. The quadratic dependence on the quencher concentration is expected to lead to a positive deviation in the SV plot, as observed in the present G-CD-NBz system.

The upward deviations in the steady-state SV plot can also arise due to transient quenching (ultrafast quenching) effects, wherein there is no actual formation of any ground state complex. However, if a quencher molecule happens to be in the vicinity of the excited emitter immediately after photoexcitation then an apparent static quenching can take place leading to instantaneous quenching of the closely placed emitter-quencher pair. In this situation, the quenching process is treated following the “sphere of action” model, and the SV Eq. (4) is modified as,
(7)
I 0
I
=
1 +
K D
Q
exp
K T
Q

Here
K T
=
N A
V
1000
=
N A
1000
4 3
π
R 3
, where V is the volume and R is the radius of the sphere around the emitter, where the probability of quenching is unity [25]. To be mentioned that unlike dynamic quenching, transient quenching does not involve any diffusional encounters between the emitter and quencher, but rather occurs in the emitter-quencher pairs that pre-exist within the quenching sphere at the moment of excitation, and takes place in the ultrafast time scales, much faster than the diffusion rate of the reactants.

The fitted curves according to Eq. (6) (green) and Eq. (7) (red), for the steady-state quenching of G-CD luminescence by NBz, are shown in Fig. 2D. It is clear that the “sphere of action” model for transient quenching (Eq. (7)) provides a significantly better fit for the observed data compared to static quenching (Eq. (6)). This indicates that transient quenching may be the major mechanism that is operative for the G-CD-NBz system. From the fitted curve following Eq. (7), the K T value is obtained as 124 M−1 (keeping the value of the dynamic quenching constant, K D, same as the K SV value obtained from the linear time-resolved SV plot, Eq. (5)). Accordingly, the radius of the quenching sphere (R) is calculated to be about 36 Å. Considering that the reported size of G-CD is ~21 Å, [24] and the approximate size of NBz is ~6 Å (from Edwards volume addition method) [39], this estimated value of R seems quite reasonable. Thus, for the G-CD-NBz system, the radius of the sphere within which the quenching probability is unity, is indicated to lie approximately in a range that is about 2.6 times greater than the contact distance between the carbon dots and the quencher (~13.5 Å).

It may be mentioned that transient quenching, as inferred for the G-CD-NBz system, can also logically lead to some nonlinearity in the SV plot obtained from time-resolved quenching. However, as discussed before, the time-resolved SV plot obtained for the G-CD-NBz system is quite linear in nature at least for the studied concentration range of the quencher (cf. Fig. 2C). That no nonlinear behavior is indicated in the present case is attributed to the finite time resolution of the TCSPC setup we used for the time-resolved measurements (IRF of the instrument is about 185 ps at the FWHM). Due to this limited time resolution, it is quite likely that the ultrafast transient quenching components that are present in the G-CD-NBz system remain undetected in the observed decays [40]. Consequently, the observation of only the relatively slower quenching components arising due to the diffusion of the reactants leads to an effectively linear correlation in the ⟨τ⟩0/⟨τ⟩ versus [NBz] plots for G-CD.

Furthermore, as suggested by Stevens, in the presence of transient quenching, the plot of
Q
I 0
I
− 1
versus [Q]1/3 should be a straight line with a negative slope that is a function of the coefficient of relative diffusion of the emitter and the quencher [38,41]. Indeed, the plot of
NBz
I 0
I
− 1
versus [NBz]1/3, for the steady-state quenching of G-CD luminescence by NBz, is found to follow a linear correlation with a negative slope, as depicted in Fig. 2E. Overall, these results provide support to the proposition that the quenching of the luminescence of G-CD by NBz occurs due to both transient quenching effects and dynamic quenching interactions, but without involving any static quenching caused by ground state complex formation.

The PL spectra and the PL decay traces of L-CD in the presence of increasing concentrations of NBz are depicted in Fig. 3A and B, respectively. As in the case of G-CD, significant quenching is observed in the steady-state intensities and the time-resolved decays for the L-CD-NBz system as well. The SV plot based on time-resolved quenching is linear while the SV plot based on the steady-state quenching shows significant upward curvature. From the linear SV plot, the bimolecular quenching rate constant, k q,lifetime, is estimated to be 9 × 109 M−1 s−1, which is well within the expected diffusion controlled limit.

Like the G-CD-NBz system, the steady-state quenching data for L-CD-NBz, which shows an upward curvature at higher quencher concentrations, can be fitted reasonably well following the “sphere of action” model for transient quenching (Eq. (7)) rather than by following the model for static quenching (Eq. (6)), as depicted in Fig. 3D. The plot of
NBz
I 0
I
− 1
versus [NBz]1/3, for the steady-state quenching of L-CD luminescence by NBz, is also found to be linear with a negative slope (Fig. 3E), which further supports the occurrence of transient quenching in the L-CD-NBz system. From the fitted curve following Eq. (7), the K T value for this emitter-quencher pair is obtained as 591 M−1 and accordingly, the radius of the quenching sphere (R) is calculated to be about 62 Å. Again, considering that the reported size of L-CD is ~70 Å [15], and the size of NBz is ~6 Å [39], the estimated value of R is observed to be approximately 1.6 times greater than the contact distance between the L-CD carbon dots and the quencher, which is quite in accordance with the “sphere of action” model of quenching.

Unlike the case of the NBz quencher, no changes were observed in the PL properties of either L-CD or G-CD, on addition of Hg2+ ions to the aqueous dispersions of the carbon dots in the acidic pH of the solutions (pH ~2.6). Quite interestingly, however, under alkaline condition (pH ~8.9), substantial quenching was observed for the L-CD-Hg2+ system [15], although no significant quenching was observed for the G-CD-Hg2+ system. Fig. 4A shows the PL spectra of L-CD with gradually increasing concentrations of Hg2+ (pH ~8.9). The decrease in the PL intensity is accompanied by a systematic increase in the PL decay rates of L-CD (Fig. 4B). The PL decay parameters of L-CD in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ ions at pH ~8.9 are presented in Table 2 .

To understand the differential behavior of L-CD and G-CD toward the Hg2+ ions, we reiterate some of the results of our previous study with L-CD. In the previous paper [15], we reported that at high pH, the functional groups on the surface of L-CD are deprotonated (Note, SI, ESM), and accordingly, at high pH, the zeta potential of L-CD becomes significantly negative (ξ = −7.8 mV at pH 9), as compared to the zeta potential in acidic conditions (ξ = 0.13 mV at pH 2.6). The formation of deprotonated species of L-CD is supported by the pH induced changes in the absorption and emission spectra of L-CD (Fig. S1, ESM). Thus, substantial electrostatic interaction is expected between Hg2+ and the negatively charged L-CD on increasing the pH of the solutions (~8.9), which apparently facilitates the quenching of the carbon dot by the metal ion. In the light of these results, we measured the zeta potential of G-CD, under acidic and basic pH conditions, and found that its zeta potential remains close to zero irrespective of the solution pH. That no deprotonated species of G-CD are formed at high pH is also supported by the similar absorption and emission features of G-CD under acidic and basic conditions (Fig. S2, ESM). Therefore, considering the overall results, that the quenching of L-CD by Hg2+ occurs only at high pH condition, when the carbon dot has a negatively charged surface, and no significant quenching is observed for G-CD, whose surface charge remains close to zero even at high pH, it may be inferred that at alkaline pH, the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged carbon dots and the positively charged metal ions plays the predominant role in the PL quenching process of L-CD-Hg2+ system. Following the inference from reported literature [34,35], the mechanism of quenching is attributed to the occurrence of electron transfer from the carbon dots to the metal ions.

For the L-CD-Hg2+ system, linear SV plots were obtained from both steady-state and time-resolved quenching studies, as depicted in Fig. 4C. It is, however, observed that the SV plot based on SS quenching (Eq. (4)) has a much higher slope compared to the SV plot based on time-resolved quenching (Eq. (5)). From the steady-state quenching data, the bimolecular quenching rate constant, k q,intensity, is determined to be 2.1 × 1011 M−1 s−1, while from the time-resolved quenching data, the bimolecular quenching rate constant, k q,lifetime, is found to be 3.7 × 1010 M−1 s−1. The significantly higher slope of the steady-state SV plot and consequently the higher value obtained for k q,intensity compared to k q,lifetime, clearly indicates the presence of a substantial contribution of transient/static quenching in the present system, in addition to the dynamic quenching, although no upward curvature is apparently observed in the steady-state SV plot for the studied concentration range of the quencher.

As discussed before, the electrostatic effect would expectedly lead to strong association between the metal ions and the L-CD carbon dots at pH 8.9. This association may be further supported by metal-ligand type of coordination between the deprotonated functional groups present at the surface of L-CD and Hg2+ [33,34,42]. Therefore, it is anticipated that static quenching rather than transient quenching may be contributing more dominantly in the interaction of the L-CD-Hg2+ system. Indeed, it is observed that the static quenching model (Eq. (6)) provides a better fit to the observed steady-state quenching data (although the I0/I versus [Hg2+] plot is largely linear in nature) than the “sphere of action” model (Fig. 4D). Furthermore, unlike the cases of L-CD-NBz and G-CD-NBz systems, the plot of
Hg
2 +
I 0
I
− 1
versus [Hg 2+]1/3 for the steady-state quenching of L-CD by Hg2+, clearly does not show any linear correlation with a negative slope (Fig. 4E), which supports that transient quenching is not that dominant in this system. From the fitted curve following Eq. (6) (cf. Fig. 4D), the value of the static quenching constant K s is determined to be 694 M−1 (keeping the value of the dynamic quenching constant, K D, fixed at the K SV value obtained from the linear time-resolved SV plot, Eq. (5)).

Since the static quenching constant is in essence a measure of the ground state association between L-CD and Hg2+, it can be expressed by the binding equilibrium as,
(8)
L − CD + n
Hg
2 +
⇔
K S
L − CD ∙
Hg
2 +
n

Assuming that the binding sites of Hg2+ on L-CD are similar, and that the binding to each of the “n” sites occurs independently, the binding constant and the binding stoichiometry can be estimated from the fluorescence intensity changes using the equation [43–45],
(9)
log
I 0
− I
I
= log
K S
+ nlog
Hg
2 +

The linear correlation and the fitted curve following Eq. (9) for the L-CD-Hg2+ system are depicted in Fig. 5 . From the fitted curve, K s is determined to be 708 M−1, which is quite close to the value obtained from the fitting of the steady-state quenching data with the modified SV equation (Eq. (6), cf. Fig. 4D, K s = 694 M−1). This result is in clear support of the proposition that static quenching contributes dominantly in the interaction of the L-CD-Hg2+ system. The value of n is found to be 0.94, which suggests that the interaction stoichiometry is 1:1.

It may further be noted that unlike the dynamic quenching rate constants (k q,lifetime) for the G-DC-NBz and L-DC-NBz systems, which are close to the expected bimolecular diffusion controlled rate, the value of k q,lifetime for the L-CD-Hg2+ system is significantly higher (k q,lifetime = 3.7 × 1010 M−1 s−1) than the typical bimolecular diffusion controlled rate (~1 × 1010 M−1 s−1) in aqueous medium, especially involving neutral reactants. This is in accordance with the strong influence of electrostatic interaction even in the dynamic quenching process of L-CD-Hg2+ system, under high pH condition. It has been realised for many systems that when the interacting species are oppositely charged, the electrostatic attraction can enhance the mutual diffusional rate of the reactants as they approach each other, and accordingly, the quenching rate constant for oppositely charged emitter-quencher pairs can exceed the typical diffusion limited rate constant encountered for neutral emitter-quencher pairs [46–48].

From the slope of the SV plot obtained from the steady-state measurements, the limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ by L-CD is determined to be 36 μM, following Eq. (10) [31].
(10)
LOD = 3 σ /
K SV
where σ is the standard deviation of 10 measurements repeated under same experimental conditions with L-CD in the absence of Hg2+ and K SV is the slope of the SV plot (cf. Eq. (4)).

It is pertinent to mention here that apart from the PL based sensing applications of carbon dot systems, these materials are also widely used as labels for bioimaging, for in vivo sensing and image guided therapy [49–53]. The major advantage of carbon dots for biological applications is their high biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Although the UV excitable carbon dots pose limitations for imaging due to the autofluorescence and photodamage of biological substrates under UV excitation light, the excitation wavelength dependent PL property of carbon dots and their synthetic modifications by doping, allow tunability of carbon dot PL toward visible regions and make them promising candidates for cellular labeling [52,53]. We were thus encouraged to examine the suitability of the present carbon dots for bioimaging (Note S2). Chinese hamster ovary cells were incubated with the studied carbon dots and were observed under a fluorescence microscope. To avoid autofluorescence, the cells were excited with blue light instead of UV light. Under these conditions, bright fluorescence images were observed for the cells labeled especially with G-CD sample, which exhibits significant excitation wavelength dependent PL behavior (cf. Fig. 1). Clear images, however, could not be observed with the L-CD sample. The fluorescence images are presented in Fig. S3, ESM, to indicate the potential of G-CD for labeling live cells.

In summary, the present study provides insights on the photophysics and luminescence quenching of carbon dots synthesized from a bioresource (L-CD) and from a small molecule precursor (G-CD), by representative quenchers, NBz and Hg2+. The observed results indicate that the PL of both carbon dots is significantly reduced in the presence of NBz, due to transient quenching and dynamic quenching interactions. The quenching by Hg2+ ions is observed only for the L-CD-Hg2+ system at high pH conditions. In this case, the interaction is revealed to be strongly influenced by electrostatic effects, leading to both static quenching and dynamic quenching, the rate constant of the latter being higher than the typical bimolecular diffusion-controlled rate (~1 × 1010 M−1 s−1) in aqueous medium, especially involving neutral reactants.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the generous support provided by the host institute, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC), Mumbai. We thank Mr. J. M. Chethodil and Dr. Praseetha P. K., Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, Tamil Nadu, for preparation of the L-CD sample, and Ms. P. Verma and Dr. A. Kunwar, BARC, for helping with the cellular labeling study. P. M. Gharat acknowledges BARC-Mumbai University joint research initiative for his research fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption and emission spectra of L-CD and G-CD under acidic and basic conditions, and cellular labeling studies.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.10.029.
